summary: I was born in Laos in 1978, but due to the war, my family had to leave when I was very young. Laos was my first home, but the conflict forced us to move to Thailand in 1980. Growing up, I often thought about my birthplace and the life we left behind.

After leaving Laos in 1980 because of the war, my family and I lived in Thailand for several years. We stayed in the Ban Vinai refugee camp, where I attended school learning Thai, Laos, and basic English. Life there was difficult, especially witnessing the Thai police treating Hmong people harshly, which made me wonder about General Vang Pao's role during those times.

Ban Vinai refugee camp in Thailand was where my family lived after fleeing Laos in 1980. I have vivid memories of running through its dusty fields with friends and attending school there. Despite the hardships, including witnessing violence against Hmong people, it was a place where I began learning languages and adapting to a new life.

In 1989, my family resettled in California, marking the start of a new chapter in my life. Arriving as a new immigrant, I was placed in 7th grade and faced many challenges adjusting to the education system and culture. California was where I first experienced the broader world beyond my village and refugee camp.

After years of working and raising my family in Minnesota, I returned to school at Anoka-Ramsey Community College. Starting college was tough; I struggled with the coursework and balancing family responsibilities, but with my husband's support, I persevered. I eventually earned my Associate's degree in Accounting there, which was a proud achievement.

Following my success at Anoka-Ramsey, I continued my education at Metropolitan State University to pursue a Bachelor's degree in Accounting. This step was important to me as I wanted to further my knowledge and share my story to inspire others. It represented hope and the possibility of education despite earlier challenges.

My family and I settled in Minnesota after arriving in the United States, and it has been our home since. It was here that I balanced work, family, and eventually returned to school. Minnesota provided the resources and opportunities that helped me pursue my education later in life.

Growing up in the Ban Vinai refugee camp, I often wondered about General Vang Pao and why he couldn't help us when we faced violence from Thai police. His name was well-known among the Hmong community, and I looked to him as a leader during those difficult times. That curiosity stayed with me as I navigated my early life in the camp.
